text: Presenting a case of laparoscopic DI resection in a patient who was in GnRH agonist therapy. This lady has history of previous laparoscopic resection of ovarian cyst for endometrioma bilaterally and following that patient evidently had a great deal of pelvic pain. She was placed to oral contraceptive for a while and then was changed to GnRH agonist therapy. She had nine injections. At about month of ten, she came to us with excruciating pelvic pain and evidently failed treatment. This patient was put to sleep before I get to see her by our fellows. And I will tell you later on why, but I usually do not. operate the patient who was recently treated with medical therapy, even oral contraceptive for resection of DIA because it practically destroys the plane of dissection.

The video shows the ovary completely adhered due to previous surgery, with visible damage and areas of endometriosis. The deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIA) is seen invading the ovary and the rectosigmoid colon. The interface between the lesion and normal tissue is obscured by fibrosis, complicating the identification of clear resection margins.

The surgeon observes that the ovary is attached to the surrounding tissue, with lesions visible on the surface of the fallopian tube. The lesions appear as deeply fibrotic tissue, complicating the identification of clear resection margins.

That is why all those patients that are coming for treatment of endometrioma laparoscopically, they should not be on any kind of medical treatment, especially GnRH agonist treatment. Simply because, number one, it makes the Enucleation of the endometrium are difficult. And number two, you take excessive normal ovarian tissue, which makes the AMH drop down remarkably. But if those people, they are not in medical treatment, then the situation is much, much better because you can do a much better clean surgery with a clear margin.

The surgeon navigates to the paracervical space on the left side, encountering fibrotic peritoneal tissue. In the caudal region, there is a deep lesion with indistinct margins. The rectovaginal space is accessed, revealing the rectum attached to a thickened peritoneum, suggesting extensive endometriosis. The unclear lesion boundaries complicate the resection process.

During the hysterectomy procedure, the surgeon navigates the pararectal space on the left side and enters the rectovaginal space. The deep lesion is visible, penetrating the rectosigmoid colon. The surgeon attempts to resect tissue from the posterior cervix, despite challenges in achieving complete resection due to fibrotic tissue and previous medical treatments.

The surgeon observes a lesion on the anterior rectum, noting the difficulty in assessing its size and extent due to fibrotic tissue. The laparoscopic procedure involves removing deep lesions from the posterior cul-de-sac.

Really, it's very difficult to say it's endometriotic lesion or not, but, of course, pathology showed that, indeed, it was endometriotic lesion. But, again, you never know whether it's been the deep lesion and also normal tissue is that you can make it decent resection, but we are trying to do our best to take as much as we can. In regard to bowel, I really don't know how bowel is involved. We informed the family and we told them if this patient comes back with bowel syndrome along the line, he's going to have to come back unfortunately and be subjected to another surgery. The patient is young, about twenty-four or twenty-five years old, and We'll see how the thing goes.

The surgeon resects a lesion on the right side, carefully locating the ureter and assessing the depth of the lesion near the peritoneum. The interface between the lesion and normal tissue is obscured. A suspension suture from the ovary is removed, revealing a deep lesion in the ovary, which is excised. The other ovary appears undamaged.
